summary: Cathy O'Neil enthusiastically recommends her friend Ernie Davis's book 'Mathematics, Substance and Surmise,' a collection of essays exploring the ontology of mathematics from multiple perspectives. She admits she hasn't finished it yet but couldn't contain her excitement. The book covers topics ranging from mathematical collaboration to computer-assisted proofs to the fundamental question of whether mathematical objects exist. O'Neil connects it to her own philosophical musings about truth and precision, using her provocative claim that 'the sun goes around the earth' as an example of how statements can be imprecise yet true. She closes with a genuine invitation for the book's contributors to come discuss mathematics at her home.
key_points: The book is a collection of essays on mathematical ontology written by multiple authors, edited by Ernie Davis and his father
It explores how mathematical collaboration has changed, how computers have become partners in mathematical discovery, and whether mathematical objects truly exist
O'Neil connects the book to her theory that precision and truth are not the same thing - a less precise statement isn't necessarily wrong
The essays provide substantive mathematical context while prompting readers to think differently about foundational questions
O'Neil's enthusiasm is so genuine she extends an open dinner invitation to all the book's contributors
money_quotes: Bloggers are notoriously bad at impulse control
What does it mean to 'know' a formula for the 4th power of pi is true without having a proof for it?
Is the number 2 a noun or an adjective? Is mathematics about the world or only about itself?

It's imprecise yet true, and that when someone corrects me and tells me the earth goes around the sun, that becomes another statement which is of course more precise and accurate statement but still not entirely precise and accurate!
So why am I wrong and they're right? What do humans mean when they say something's right, anyway?
The book consistently makes you think differently, while also giving you substantive and grounded mathematical context in which to do your thinking
key_insight: Mathematical truth and precision are not synonymous - a statement can be imprecise yet true, raising fundamental questions about what it means to be 'right' about anything.
